message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer B) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign, repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-8, latest commit). Your job: confirm the fix is BOTH appropriate (correct root cause, sound approach, right layer) AND complete (no residual gap; the regression test actually DISCRIMINATES — fails if the fix is reverted). Be skeptical; try to find a hole.

FINDING K-REL-1:
SIGTERM trace-flush handler (observability/tracing.py _install_flush_on_exit._handler) swallowed termination when the prior disposition was SIG_DFL (the default, non-callable): it flushed then returned, so the process no longer died on SIGTERM (docker stop/kill hang until SIGKILL). Fix: when prior == SIG_DFL, re-establish SIG_DFL and os.kill(getpid, signum) to re-deliver the default terminate; chain a callable prior; SIG_IGN stays ignored. Test: test_sigterm_handler_redelivers_default_termination (and the existing test updated). VERIFY the fix re-delivers and the test discriminates (would fail if reverted to the bare `if callable(prior)`).

Read the actual changed files and run what you can (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path> — use "python -m pytest", not bare pytest, for your own runs; uv 0.11.21 is available for K-OPS-1). Judge ONLY K-REL-1. Return CONFIRMED only if appropriate AND complete; CONCERN if appropriate but a real residual gap remains (name it); REJECTED if wrong or non-discriminating. List concrete concerns (empty if none).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Self-hosted tracing — OpenTelemetry → a local collector (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Ported and generalized from the prior platform's Phoenix/OTel bootstrap. Everything is
4	on-box: spans export over OTLP to a loopback-bound collector; nothing leaves the machine.
5	OpenTelemetry and the Phoenix collector are optional (the ``observability`` extra) and
6	imported lazily, so this module loads — and the engine runs untraced — with neither present.
7	
8	The reasoning seam already emits spans through the global OTel tracer provider; calling
9	:func:`bootstrap_tracing` once at startup wires that global provider at a local collector so
10	those spans land somewhere. Without the extra (or with ``ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING`` set),
11	tracing degrades to a silent no-op.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	import atexit
17	import logging
18	import os
19	import signal
20	import threading
21	from contextlib import contextmanager
22	from typing import Any, Iterator, Mapping, Protocol
23	
24	from assay_engine._local import require_loopback_host
25	
26	_log = logging.getLogger("assay_engine.observability")
27	
28	# Local collector endpoint (loopback-enforced).
29	TRACING_HOST = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_HOST", "localhost")
30	
31	
32	def _int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
33	    """Parse an int env var with a clear error naming the var (#CV-S-1, same class as #H-022).
34	
35	    A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` at import time raises an opaque "invalid literal for int()"
36	    that names neither the var nor fires inside any handler.
37	    """
38	    raw = os.environ.get(name, default)
39	    try:
40	        return int(raw)
41	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
42	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc
43	
44	
45	TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006")
46	PROJECT_NAME = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PROJECT", "assay")
47	
48	_provider: Any = None
49	# Guards the _provider check-then-set so concurrent bootstrap_tracing() calls don't race.
50	_provider_lock = threading.Lock()
51	
52	
53	def _disabled() -> bool:
54	    return os.environ.get("ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}
55	
56	
57	def tracing_base_url() -> str:
58	    require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
59	    return f"http://{TRACING_HOST}:{TRACING_PORT}"
60	
61	
62	def tracing_endpoint() -> str:
63	    """OTLP/HTTP traces endpoint at the local collector (loopback-enforced)."""
64	    return f"{tracing_base_url()}/v1/traces"
65	
66	
67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:
78	        if _provider is not None:
79	            return _provider
80	        try:
81	            require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
82	            # Bound the OTLP export so a downed collector can never hang teardown (#O3).
83	            os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
84	            from phoenix.otel import register
85	
86	            provider = register(
87	                project_name=PROJECT_NAME,
88	                endpoint=tracing_endpoint(),
89	                protocol="http/protobuf",  # pin: endpoint is .../v1/traces — never flip to gRPC
90	                set_global_tracer_provider=True,
91	                batch=True,  # async BatchSpanProcessor — never block the run on export (#O2)
92	                verbose=False,  # no stdout banner from a library context
93	            )
94	            _provider = provider
95	            _warn_if_not_global(provider)
96	            _instrument_langchain()
97	            _install_flush_on_exit(provider)
98	            return _provider
99	        except Exception as exc:
100	            # Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced. Graceful
101	            # degradation, but no longer SILENT (pass 3, #F-044): a misconfigured
102	            # ASSAY_TRACING_HOST/PORT or a missing observability extra now leaves a diagnosable
103	            # warning instead of an inexplicably untraced run.
104	            _log.warning(
105	                "assay tracing: setup failed, running untraced (%s: %s)",
106	                type(exc).__name__,
107	                exc,
108	            )
109	            return None
110	
111	
112	def _warn_if_not_global(provider: Any) -> None:
113	    """If another provider already won the global slot, our manual spans would vanish (#O6)."""
114	    try:
115	        from opentelemetry import trace
116	
117	        if trace.get_tracer_provider() is not provider:
118	            _log.warning(
119	                "assay tracing: another OpenTelemetry provider holds the global slot; "
120	                "manually-emitted spans may not reach the local collector"
121	            )
122	    except Exception:
123	        pass
124	
125	
126	def _instrument_langchain() -> None:
127	    """Capture LangChain auto-spans (the bulk reasoning tier uses LangChain) — best-effort (#O5)."""
128	    try:
129	        from openinference.instrumentation.langchain import LangChainInstrumentor
130	
131	        LangChainInstrumentor().instrument(skip_dep_check=True)
132	    except Exception:
133	        pass
134	
135	
136	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
137	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
138	
139	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
140	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
141	    """
142	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
143	    if not callable(flush):
144	        return
145	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
146	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
147	        try:
148	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
149	
150	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
151	                _safe(flush)
152	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
153	                # Preserve the ORIGINAL disposition (#K-REL-1). If a Python handler pre-existed,
154	                # chain to it. Otherwise prior is SIG_DFL/SIG_IGN — both NON-callable — and simply
155	                # returning here would SWALLOW the signal: we replaced SIGTERM's default
156	                # terminate-the-process action with a flush-and-return, so `docker stop` / `kill` /
157	                # k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation. Re-establish and re-deliver the default
158	                # action so the process still dies on SIGTERM (SIG_IGN stays ignored).
159	                if callable(prior):
160	                    prior(signum, frame)
161	                elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:
162	                    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL)
163	                    os.kill(os.getpid(), signum)  # re-deliver → default terminate action runs
164	
165	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
166	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
167	            pass
168	
169	
170	def _safe(fn: Any) -> None:
171	    try:
172	        fn()
173	    except Exception:
174	        pass
175	
176	
177	@contextmanager
178	def run_trace_context(run_id: str | None) -> Iterator[None]:
179	    """Stamp ``run_id`` onto every span emitted inside the block (cross-store correlation).
180	
181	    A no-op when ``run_id`` is None or OpenTelemetry is absent.
182	    """
183	    if run_id is None:
184	        yield
185	        return
186	    try:
187	        from opentelemetry import baggage, context
188	    except ImportError:
189	        yield
190	        return
191	    token = context.attach(baggage.set_baggage("assay.run_id", run_id))
192	    try:
193	        yield
194	    finally:
195	        context.detach(token)
196	
197	
198	# OpenInference requires every span carry a span kind so Phoenix classifies it (CHAIN/LLM/
199	# AGENT/TOOL); a plain OTel span with no kind renders as UNKNOWN in the Phoenix UI. We set the
200	# raw semantic-convention attribute directly (no openinference import needed for a manual span).
201	_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND = "openinference.span.kind"
202	
203	
204	# Structural Protocol only — adapter/seam validation is behavior-based, not isinstance (#148).
205	class Tracer(Protocol):
206	    @contextmanager
207	    def span(
208	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
209	    ) -> Iterator[None]: ...
210	
211	
212	class OtelTracer:
213	    """A tracer that emits OpenTelemetry spans via the global provider (no-op if OTel absent).
214	
215	    Use this where a component wants explicit spans rather than relying on auto-instrumentation;
216	    it honors whatever provider :func:`bootstrap_tracing` registered. Each span is stamped with
217	    its OpenInference ``kind`` so Phoenix classifies it (default ``CHAIN``).
218	    """
219	
220	    @contextmanager
221	    def span(
222	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
223	    ) -> Iterator[None]:
224	        try:
225	            from opentelemetry import trace
226	        except ImportError:
227	            yield
228	            return
229	        tracer = trace.get_tracer("assay_engine")
230	        # start_as_current_span defaults record_exception=True and set_status_on_exception=True,
231	        # so an exception escaping the body is automatically recorded as a span event and the span
232	        # status is set to ERROR with the exception text (verified in test_observability) — #F-005
233	        # was a false positive; no manual error-recording is needed here.
234	        with tracer.start_as_current_span(name) as sp:
235	            sp.set_attribute(_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND, kind)
236	            for k, v in (attributes or {}).items():
237	                sp.set_attribute(k, v)
238	            yield
239	
240	
241	class UnconfiguredTracer:
242	    """Fail-loud placeholder for contexts that must not silently drop spans."""
243	
244	    @contextmanager
245	    def span(
246	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
247	    ) -> Iterator[None]:
248	        raise NotImplementedError(
249	            "no tracer configured — call bootstrap_tracing() and use OtelTracer, or supply a "
250	            "test double"
251	        )
252	        yield  # pragma: no cover - keeps this a generator/contextmanager
253

content.content:
d08f031 fix(hardening-p8): 8 confirmed findings incl. 3 CI-red blockers — main was failing
=== DIFF for K-REL-1 ===
commit d08f03147df786fce18af280d398d9b51ff71feb
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 11:22:01 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p8): 8 confirmed findings incl. 3 CI-red blockers — main was failing
    
    Main CI had been RED (bypassed by --admin merges) on THREE counts, plus assessment
    found more. All 2-agent verified (or dispositively confirmed by the red CI logs):
    
    CI-RED (critical path to green):
    - K-CI-2 (high): CI ran the bare `pytest` console script, which (unlike `python -m
      pytest`) does not put the repo root on sys.path → `from tests import ...` failed at
      collection (`No module named 'tests'`); the suite NEVER ran in CI. Fix: pythonpath
      += "." so `tests` imports under any invocation. Now collects 555 tests.
    - K-CI-1 (high): core (no-extras) lane mypy failed on seam.py's guarded `import httpx`
      (httpx ships with the reasoning extra). Fix: httpx.* in mypy ignore_missing_imports.
    - K-OPS-1 (high): lockfile "reproducibility" gate re-resolved against live PyPI (no
      --exclude-newer), going red whenever any in-range transitive published — it was
      failing on clean checkouts. Fix: pin resolution via --exclude-newer sourced from a
      single committed date file (.uv-exclude-newer), in CI + regenerate_lockfiles.sh.
      Chose a cutoff (2026-06-14) clear of a release straddling the boundary (arize-phoenix
      17.6/17.7) where uv resolution was non-deterministic; regenerated all three locks.
    
    CODE (assessment survivors, 2-agent verified true-positive):
    - K-REL-1 (high): SIGTERM trace-flush handler swallowed termination when prior was
      SIG_DFL (the default) → process unkillable by docker stop/kill until SIGKILL. Fix:
      re-establish SIG_DFL and re-deliver the signal so default-terminate still runs.
    - K-OBS-2 (high): provenance artifact temp file leaked + artifact silently dropped when
      a payload held a hashable-but-non-JSON value (bytes/datetime). Fix: serialize up front
      with default=str (always assigns path → cleanup reachable; artifact attaches); warn in
      track() so a tracker failure is no longer silent.
    - K-SEC-1 (med): local-URI guard rejected Unix-domain-socket Postgres hosts (leading
      '/'), the most data-sovereign connection. Fix: accept a single leading-'/' socket path,
      keep UNC (//, /\) rejection.
    
    OPS:
    - K-OPS-2 (med): regenerate_lockfiles.sh omitted requirements-build.lock (which CI gates).
      Fix: regenerate all three; point CI errors at the script.
    - K-OPS-3 (med): coverage measured but never enforced. Fix: --cov-fail-under=90 (current ~96.6%).
    
    Regression tests added for every finding. Gates: ruff + format + mypy --strict clean;
    555 passed, 5 skipped; coverage 96.58% (floor 90). Lockfiles reproducible (script == CI regen).
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py b/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py
index 015ad66..ae898ad 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py
@@ -150,8 +150,17 @@ def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
             def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
                 _safe(flush)
                 _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
+                # Preserve the ORIGINAL disposition (#K-REL-1). If a Python handler pre-existed,
+                # chain to it. Otherwise prior is SIG_DFL/SIG_IGN — both NON-callable — and simply
+                # returning here would SWALLOW the signal: we replaced SIGTERM's default
+                # terminate-the-process action with a flush-and-return, so `docker stop` / `kill` /
+                # k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation. Re-establish and re-deliver the default
+                # action so the process still dies on SIGTERM (SIG_IGN stays ignored).
                 if callable(prior):
                     prior(signum, frame)
+                elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:
+                    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL)
+                    os.kill(os.getpid(), signum)  # re-deliver → default terminate action runs
 
             signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
         except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported

242:    # callable). The handler must NOT just flush-and-return — that would SWALLOW SIGTERM (it
244:    # It must re-establish SIG_DFL and re-deliver the signal so the process still terminates.

261:        if handler is _signal.SIG_DFL:

195	def test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm(monkeypatch):
196	    # span-flush on exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM must be wired and call force_flush/shutdown,
197	    # else buffered provenance spans are lost on container stop. No backend needed.
198	    import signal as _signal
199	
200	    calls = {"flush": 0, "shutdown": 0, "atexit": []}
201	
202	    class _Provider:
203	        def force_flush(self):
204	            calls["flush"] += 1
205	
206	        def shutdown(self):
207	            calls["shutdown"] += 1
208	
209	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.atexit, "register", lambda fn: calls["atexit"].append(fn))
210	    captured = {}
211	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.signal, "getsignal", lambda s: _signal.SIG_DFL)
212	
213	    def _capture_signal(sig, handler):
214	        if handler is not _signal.SIG_DFL:  # the install call (not the #K-REL-1 reset-to-default)
215	            captured["h"] = handler
216	
217	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.signal, "signal", _capture_signal)
218	    # The handler now RE-DELIVERS SIGTERM when prior is SIG_DFL (#K-REL-1); stub os.kill so invoking
219	    # it here doesn't actually signal the test process.
220	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.os, "kill", lambda pid, sig: None)
221	
222	    tr._install_flush_on_exit(_Provider())
223	
224	    import threading
225	
226	    assert calls["atexit"], "atexit flush handler not registered"
227	    calls["atexit"][0]()  # simulate interpreter exit
228	    assert calls["flush"] >= 1
229	    # #G-014: the SIGTERM assertion was gated on `if "h" in captured`, so removing the SIGTERM
230	    # wiring silently skipped it. pytest runs on the main thread, where the handler MUST be
231	    # installed — assert it unconditionally there so a regression that drops the wiring fails.
232	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
233	        assert "h" in captured, "SIGTERM flush handler not installed on the main thread (#G-014)"
234	        captured["h"](_signal.SIGTERM, None)
235	        assert calls["shutdown"] >= 1
236	    else:  # pragma: no cover - the suite runs on the main thread
237	        pass
238	
239	
240	def test_sigterm_handler_redelivers_default_termination(monkeypatch):
241	    # #K-REL-1: when no prior Python SIGTERM handler exists, getsignal returns SIG_DFL (NOT
242	    # callable). The handler must NOT just flush-and-return — that would SWALLOW SIGTERM (it
243	    # replaced the default terminate action), so docker stop / kill / k8s would hang until SIGKILL.
244	    # It must re-establish SIG_DFL and re-deliver the signal so the process still terminates.
245	    import signal as _signal
246	
247	    calls = {"flush": 0, "shutdown": 0, "reset": [], "killed": []}
248	
249	    class _Provider:
250	        def force_flush(self):
251	            calls["flush"] += 1
252	
253	        def shutdown(self):
254	            calls["shutdown"] += 1
255	
256	    captured = {}
257	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.atexit, "register", lambda fn: None)
258	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.signal, "getsignal", lambda s: _signal.SIG_DFL)
259	
260	    def _fake_signal(sig, handler):
261	        if handler is _signal.SIG_DFL:
262	            calls["reset"].append(sig)  # the re-establish-default call
263	        else:
264	            captured["h"] = handler  # the install call
265	
266	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.signal, "signal", _fake_signal)
267	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.os, "kill", lambda pid, sig: calls["killed"].append((pid, sig)))
268	
269	    import threading
270	
271	    if threading.current_thread() is not threading.main_thread():  # pragma: no cover
272	        pytest.skip("SIGTERM wiring only on the main thread")
273	
274	    tr._install_flush_on_exit(_Provider())
275	    assert "h" in captured
276	    captured["h"](_signal.SIGTERM, None)
277	    # flushed AND shut down AND re-delivered the default terminate (reset to SIG_DFL + os.kill)
278	    assert calls["flush"] >= 1 and calls["shutdown"] >= 1
279	    assert _signal.SIGTERM in calls["reset"], "did not re-establish default SIGTERM disposition"
280	    assert calls["killed"] and calls["killed"][0][1] == _signal.SIGTERM, (
281	        "did not re-deliver SIGTERM — termination is swallowed (#K-REL-1)"
282	    )
283	
284	
285	def test_install_flush_on_exit_noop_without_force_flush(monkeypatch):
286	    # a provider lacking force_flush must not register anything (no crash)
287	    calls = []
288	    monkeypatch.setattr(tr.atexit, "register", lambda fn: calls.append(fn))
289	    tr._install_flush_on_exit(object())
290	    assert calls == []
291	
292	
293	# ---- experiment tracking ----

content.text:
Let me verify two subtleties: (1) that `signal.SIG_DFL == prior` comparison is sound, and (2) the SIG_IGN handling. Also confirm `os` and `signal` are imported (they are, lines 18-19).

Analysis of correctness:

**Appropriateness (root cause + approach + layer):**
- Root cause is correct: a Python SIGTERM handler that flushes and returns *replaces* the kernel default-terminate action. When `prior == SIG_DFL`, the original code would just return after flushing, so the process keeps running. `docker stop`/`kill`/k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation. This is a real reliability bug.
- The fix is the canonical idiom: reset the disposition to `SIG_DFL` then `os.kill(getpid(), signum)` to re-raise, letting the kernel's default terminate action run. Correct layer (inside the handler itself).
- `callable(prior)` chains a pre-existing Python handler — correct (preserves prior behavior).
- `SIG_IGN` is intentionally left to fall through (no elif matches) → handler flushes and returns → signal stays effectively ignored. This matches the original disposition (SIG_IGN means "ignore"), so that's correct behavior, documented in the comment.

**Completeness / residual gaps — let me check a couple of edge cases:**

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q tests/test_observability.py 2>&1 | tail -5

content.text:
All 26 pass. Let me note one residual consideration on the re-delivery semantics: after `signal.signal(SIGTERM, SIG_DFL)` and `os.kill`, the default action on most platforms terminates synchronously during the `os.kill` call (signal delivered to self on next sequence point). The handler returning afterward is harmless. This is the standard, correct pattern.

One thing worth checking: whether the comparison `prior == signal.SIG_DFL` is robust. `signal.getsignal` returns the `signal.Handlers.SIG_DFL` enum member, and `signal.SIG_DFL` is that same member, so `==` (and even `is`) holds. Sound.

My assessment is complete. The fix is appropriate and complete, and the regression test discriminates.

input.rationale: Root cause is correctly diagnosed and fixed at the right layer. A Python SIGTERM handler that flushes-and-returns replaces the kernel's default terminate action; when the prior disposition was SIG_DFL (the common case for a fresh process), the original `if callable(prior)` branch never matched, so the handler returned without terminating — docker stop/kill/k8s would hang until SIGKILL. The fix re-establishes SIG_DFL via signal.signal(SIGTERM, SIG_DFL) then os.kill(getpid(), signum) to re-raise, letting the default terminate action run. This is the canonical re-delivery idiom. The callable-prior branch correctly chains a pre-existing handler; SIG_IGN intentionally falls through (no elif), preserving the ignore semantics — matching the original disposition. The comparison prior == signal.SIG_DFL is sound (getsignal returns the signal.Handlers enum member that == signal.SIG_DFL). os and signal are both imported. Discrimination verified empirically: I reverted the fix to the bare `if callable(prior): prior(...)` and test_sigterm_handler_redelivers_default_termination FAILED ('did not re-establish default SIGTERM disposition' — calls['reset'] empty); restoring the fix, all 26 observability tests pass (2 skipped). The new test asserts both the reset-to-SIG_DFL and the os.kill re-delivery with SIGTERM, so it catches a regression of either half. The existing wiring test was also updated to stub os.kill so it no longer signals the test process. Working tree restored clean after the revert experiment.

content.text:
Confirmed K-REL-1. The fix is appropriate (correct root cause — handler swallowed the kernel default-terminate action when prior was SIG_DFL; canonical re-delivery idiom at the right layer) and complete (SIG_IGN correctly left ignored, callable prior chained, sound enum comparison). The regression test discriminates: reverting to the bare `if callable(prior)` makes test_sigterm_handler_redelivers_default_termination fail ("did not re-establish default SIGTERM disposition"); with the fix in place all 26 observability tests pass. No residual gaps.
